eli3: Cross-entropy measures the 'distance' between two probability distributions. It's H(p,q) = -Σ p(x) log q(x). Think of it as: if the true distribution is p, how surprised are you on average if you believe distribution q?
Cross-entropy combines two things: the inherent uncertainty of p (its entropy) plus the 'penalty' for using q instead of p (KL divergence). So H(p,q) = H(p) + KL(p||q).
In classification, we minimize cross-entropy loss between true labels (one-hot p) and predicted probabilities (softmax q). This forces predictions to match the true distribution.

whenWhy.title: Classification loss functions
whenWhy.body: Cross-entropy loss (log loss) is standard for multi-class classification: L = -Σ yᵢ log(ŷᵢ). Penalizes confident wrong predictions heavily. Outperforms MSE for classification because it matches probability interpretation and has better gradients.

whenWhy.title: Language modeling
whenWhy.body: Perplexity = exp(cross-entropy). Measures how well a language model predicts next words. Lower cross-entropy = lower perplexity = better model. Used to evaluate GPT, BERT, and all language models.

whenWhy.title: Generative models
whenWhy.body: VAEs use reconstruction loss (often MSE or cross-entropy) plus KL regularization. GANs implicitly minimize Jensen-Shannon divergence (symmetrized KL). Diffusion models use cross-entropy-like objectives for noise prediction.

whenWhy.title: Multi-label and hierarchical classification
whenWhy.body: Binary cross-entropy for each label independently. Sigmoid + BCE instead of softmax + CE. Handles multiple positive labels. Hierarchical cross-entropy exploits class hierarchies for better generalization.

deepDiveIntro: For distributions p (true) and q (predicted/approximating), cross-entropy is H(p,q) = -Σ_x p(x) log q(x) = E_p[-log q(X)]. For continuous: -∫ p(x) log q(x) dx. Related to KL: H(p,q) = H(p) + D(p||q) = entropy + KL divergence. Since H(p) is constant w.r.t. q, minimizing cross-entropy ≡ minimizing KL.

In ML, p is typically the empirical distribution (one-hot for classification: p(k) = 1 if k = true label, else 0). Then H(p,q) = -log q(true label). This is the negative log-likelihood for categorical distributions, justifying cross-entropy as maximum likelihood estimation.

deepDive.heading: Definition and Relation to KL
deepDive.body: H(p,q) = -Σ p(x) log q(x). H(p,q) = H(p) + D(p||q). Cross-entropy = entropy of true distribution + KL divergence from true to predicted. Minimizing H(p,q) over q is equivalent to minimizing D(p||q) since H(p) is constant.
deepDive.formula: H(p,q) = H(p) + D(p||q) = -Σ p(x) log q(x)

deepDive.heading: Binary Cross-Entropy
deepDive.body: For binary classification with labels y ∈ {0,1} and predictions p = P(Y=1): BCE = -[y log p + (1-y) log(1-p)]. Convex in p. For multiple independent labels, sum BCE over each. Used in multi-label classification, GANs (discriminator loss).

deepDive.heading: Categorical Cross-Entropy
deepDive.body: For K classes with one-hot target y and softmax probabilities ŷ: CE = -Σₖ₌₁ᴷ yₖ log(ŷₖ) = -log(ŷ_{true}). If prediction is confident and wrong (ŷ_{true} ≈ 0), loss → ∞. Numerically stable computation: subtract max(logits) before softmax.

deepDive.heading: Label Smoothing
deepDive.body: Replace one-hot targets (0,1) with smoothed (ε/(K-1), 1-ε). Prevents overconfident predictions, acts as regularization. Cross-entropy with smoothing: -(1-ε)log ŷ_{true} - Σ_{k≠true} (ε/(K-1)) log ŷₖ. Used in training transformers and image classifiers.
deepDive.formula: y'_k = 1 - ε if k = true, ε/(K-1) otherwise

deepDive.heading: Focal Loss
deepDive.body: Addresses class imbalance by down-weighting easy examples. FL = -Σ (1-ŷₖ)^γ yₖ log(ŷₖ) where γ ≥ 0. When γ=0, recovers CE. As γ increases, easy examples (high ŷₖ) contribute less. Used in object detection (RetinaNet) with extreme imbalance.

takeaways: H(p,q) = H(p) + D(p||q). Minimizing CE = minimizing KL.
Classification: CE = -log(predicted probability of true class).
Binary CE for multi-label; softmax CE for multi-class.
Label smoothing and focal loss are CE variants for regularization and imbalance.

edgeCases.q: What happens when q(x) = 0 but p(x) > 0?
edgeCases.a: Cross-entropy is infinite (∞). This means the model assigns zero probability to a true outcome, which is catastrophic. In practice, we clip probabilities (e.g., ε = 1e-7) to avoid numerical issues. This is why we don't use hard 0/1 targets in training.

edgeCases.q: Why is cross-entropy better than MSE for classification?
edgeCases.a: (1) Gradients: CE has steeper gradients when predictions are wrong, faster learning. (2) Probabilistic: CE is negative log-likelihood, MSE assumes Gaussian errors. (3) Saturation: sigmoid + MSE gradients vanish when saturated; CE gradients don't. (4) Convexity: CE loss is convex in logits; MSE isn't.

edgeCases.q: What's the difference between cross-entropy and log loss?
edgeCases.a: They're the same thing. 'Log loss' is common in competitions (Kaggle) and scikit-learn. 'Cross-entropy' is common in deep learning frameworks (PyTorch, TensorFlow). Both refer to -Σ p(x) log q(x). Binary log loss = binary cross-entropy.

sources.label: Goodfellow et al. — Deep Learning, Ch. 6
Lin et al. — Focal Loss for Dense Object Detection
